coredata.dc:title: Mechanical vibration modeling and characterization of a plastic-cased lithium-ion battery
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of Beijing Institute of Technology Press Co., Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiaoyu
Huang, Zhijia
Hua, Wen
Rao, Lalith
Tian, Yong
Tian, Jindong
coredata.dc:description: Batteries often show the coupling change of multiple physical field characteristic parameters in the charging or discharging processes. Conventional battery modeling and characterization methods mainly focus on electrical or thermal parameters. Considering that battery state change can cause a change in the mechanical structure, research on the vibration characteristic modeling and characterization of a plastic-cased lithium-ion battery is carried out. By analyzing the mechanical structure of the battery, the first principles modeling method is selected to model the battery's mechanical performance. The vibration data in the process of battery charging and discharging are measured by a laser Doppler vibrometer (LDV). The obtained experimental data are used to identify the mechanical parameters of the battery. Based on the experimentael results, the root mean square error (RMSE) of the frequency domain amplitude fitting result of the model is less than 11.36%. The effectiveness of the battery mechanical vibration model is verified. The model and the characterization method provide tools for nondestructive battery state evaluation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Mechanical vibration
Modeling
Characterization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structure of a plastic-cased lithium-ion battery
Construction of the mechanical battery model
Comparison of the vibration characteristics with different SOCs
The relationship between battery mechanical parameters and SOC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Plastic-cased lithium-ion battery and its disassembled condition.
Mechanical battery model of the experimental object.
Experimental system for the battery mechanical vibration test.
Vibration characteristic curves with different SOCs. (a) Charging process. (b) discharging process.
Results of battery vibration curves during charging with different SOCs. (a) SOC ​= ​0%. (b) SOC ​= ​20%. (c) SOC ​= ​40%. (d) SOC ​= ​60%. (e) SOC ​= ​80%. (f) SOC ​= ​100%.
Results of battery vibration curves during discharging with different SOCs. (a) SOC ​= ​100%. (b) SOC ​= ​80%. (c) SOC ​= ​60%. (d) SOC ​= ​40%. (e) SOC ​= ​20%. (f) SOC ​= ​0%.
The variation of damping parameters of battery mechanical vibration model with battery SOC. (a)∼(e) show the parameters c 1∼c 5. (f) the mean values.
The variation in stiffness parameters with battery charge and discharge in the vibration model. (a)∼(e) show the parameters k 1∼k 5. (f) the mean values.
Curves of the centroids with different SOCs. (a) Centroid 1. (b) Centroid 2. (c) Centroid 3.
Mechanical vibration model parameters of the battery under different SOCs.
The fitting error of the model to the vibration characteristics of the battery.
Correlation coefficients between the characteristic parameters and battery SOC.

head.ce:title: Mechanical vibration modeling and characterization of a plastic-cased lithium-ion battery

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy (SZ), Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy (SZ)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy (SZ), Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Batteries often show the coupling change of multiple physical field characteristic parameters in the charging or discharging processes. Conventional battery modeling and characterization methods mainly focus on electrical or thermal parameters. Considering that battery state change can cause a change in the mechanical structure, research on the vibration characteristic modeling and characterization of a plastic-cased lithium-ion battery is carried out. By analyzing the mechanical structure of the battery, the first principles modeling method is selected to model the battery's mechanical performance. The vibration data in the process of battery charging and discharging are measured by a laser Doppler vibrometer (LDV). The obtained experimental data are used to identify the mechanical parameters of the battery. Based on the experimentael results, the root mean square error (RMSE) of the frequency domain amplitude fitting result of the model is less than 11.36%. The effectiveness of the battery mechanical vibration model is verified. The model and the characterization method provide tools for nondestructive battery state evaluation.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, energy and climate issues have attracted much attention. Countries around the world have proposed plans to reduce carbon emissions. As a kind of secondary energy, electric energy has the characteristics of diverse sources and can be stored. The application of electric vehicles (EVs) has the characteristics of low carbon [1], and EVs are becoming increasingly popular with consumers [2,3]. As the largest electric vehicle market in the world, the number of EVs in China has reached 6.4 million by the end of 2021. In EVs, battery energy storage system (BESS) is widely used. The state evaluation, safety prevention and control of the BESS have attracted much attention. Battery characteristic modeling is the basis of battery system monitoring and control [4,5]. In the process of recycling after battery retirement from the electric vehicle, battery characteristic modeling plays an important role in the rapid diagnosis of battery state [6–8], which will be conducive to the classification and rational utilization.

In the traditional battery modeling process, the electrical and thermal characteristics of the battery are mainly considered [9–11]. The models are applied to the state detection of batteries in the EV applications [12,13]. However, the change of battery performance during use is multi-dimensional [14,15]. Batteries often show coupling changes in the characteristics of multiple physical fields. As one of the structural properties of the battery, the mechanical properties of the battery system can be affected by the electrical and thermal characteristic parameters [16,17]. At the same time, it also reflects the state changes inside the battery. Li et al. [18] performed indentation tests for lithium-ion batteries. The results showed that the mechanical behavior of the cylindrical cell is significantly dependent on state of charge (SOC). Xu et al. [19] regarded the lithium-ion battery as an intact structure. The mechanical behaviors with various SOCs of the whole lithium-ion battery were analyzed in terms of structure stiffness. Wang et al. [20] used tensile test to study the anode and cathode of 18650 Nickel Cobalt Aluminum Oxide (NCA)/graphite cell. The results showed that the mechanical properties of both electrodes were highly dependent on strain rate and electrolyte content. The modeling and utilization of battery mechanical characteristics began to be considered.

Yen-Jie Ee et al. [21] proposed a method for battery SOC estimation based on nonelectrical parameters and a deep neural network (DNN). The mechanical properties of the battery were exploited in the study. The strain and temperature of the battery were measured using a fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensor. The output spectrum was demodulated with pseudo-high-resolution interrogation (PHRI) method to improve the detection accuracy. Yiding Li et al. [22] investigated the multiple physical process of battery failure in lithium-ion batteries under mechanical abuse. A battery-based mechanical-electrochemical-thermal coupling model is designed. According to the study, the coupled model pointed out that the internal short-circuit heat was the main heat source for a short period of time during the hard short-circuit phase under mechanical abuse. It provided an analysis method for battery fault diagnosis. Philipp Berg et al. [23] studied the effect of random vibration on the internal structure of the battery. Eighteen different 18650 battery types were tested with two random vibration loads. The changes in the internal structure before and after the test were analyzed by computer tomography, which emphasized that the battery design should consider the mechanical vibration durability of the battery. Joel Galos et al. [24] investigated the effect of embedding batteries in carbon fiber laminates and sandwich panels on the vibrational and acoustic properties. The vibration response signals were measured using a laser Doppler vibrometer (LDV), and the structure was numerically calculated using finite element modal analysis. The results show that to control the vibrational properties, the placement of lithium polymer batteries needs to be carefully designed in the composite structure. Sung-Kwon Hong et al. [25] studied a numerical vibration model of a hybrid electric vehicle (HEV) battery pack. The influence of the prestressing on the structural characteristics of the battery pack was analyzed. The effects of random differences in structural properties between battery cells were also discussed. A parametric reduced-order model was proposed for battery pack vibration characterization.

According to the literature review, the research on the mechanical characteristics of batteries is relatively extensive. Mechanical performance analyses on battery cells and battery systems are involved. The application areas are mainly in battery system optimization, state estimation and fault diagnosis. However, the existing mechanical models are relatively complex. For example, the models proposed in literatures [10,18] are both finite element simulation models with high computational complexity and many parameters. However, in practical application, such as battery detection of electric vehicles or retired batteries, excessive model complexity will increase the cost of the system and reduce the ability of real-time response. Furthermore, there may be coupling between model parameters which makes parameter calibration difficult to be implement. The existing mechanical models are complex and difficult to apply in engineering. In addition, there is a lack of modeling research on the mechanical characteristics of plastic-cased batteries, which are also widely used in energy storage applications. Modeling the mechanical properties of a plastic-cased battery is a fundamental work for battery state evaluation, and can help establish the health assessment and the fault diagnosis when the battery is decommissioned from the EVs.

The rest of this paper is as follows: in Section 2, the structure of a plastic-cased lithium-ion battery is introduced. On this basis, the mechanical performance of the battery is modeled. Section 3 introduces the experimental system, the experiments and the data preprocessing method. In Section 4, the battery experimental and modeling results are analyzed. Section 5 presents some conclusions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure of a plastic-cased lithium-ion battery
ce:section.ce:para: A high-capacity plastic-cased LiFePO₄ battery produced by China Aviation Lithium Battery (CALB) Co., Ltd. is taken as an example. The battery sample and its disassembled condition are shown in Fig. 1 . In this section, the modeling process of the mechanical performance of the battery is introduced. After the battery is disassembled, it can be seen that the interior of the battery is composed of two battery cell components similar to the soft-packed cells. The current collectors of the two components are gathered together and lead out through two tabs. The two battery cell components are in a parallel structure. The plastic casing of the battery is attached to the two cells, and the interior of the plastic casing is filled with electrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the mechanical battery model
ce:section.ce:para: According to the structure of the battery, the battery is roughly divided into the shell and the part of the inner core, in which the part of the inner core is composed of two stacked battery cell components. The mechanical performance of the battery of the plastic shell is affected by the characteristics of each component. To model the mechanical properties of the battery, the battery structure is simplified to reduce the complexity. Herein, a viscous damping spring-mass model for battery characteristic description is established. In the model, the two battery cell components of the inner core can be approximately regarded as two interacting spring-mass blocks. The shell can be roughly regarded as a mechanical part. However, the hardness and stiffness of the plastic shell are lower than that of the metal shell, and its damping is also greater. Therefore, it is likely that the vibrations of the upper and the lower surfaces are inconsistent. In addition, when the battery is placed horizontally, the upper surface of the battery is only subjected to the force of the internal components and the surrounding shells, while the lower surface of the battery is subjected to the total mass of the battery and the excitation of vibration. Therefore, the mechanical vibration of the upper plastic shell is inconsistent with that of the lower shell. The mechanical characteristics of the battery shell are described by two interacting spring-mass blocks in the model. Then, the four-degree-of-freedom viscous damping spring-mass model of the battery is established according to the approximation process. The mechanical battery model is shown in Fig. 2 .

In the battery model, m 1 represents the mass of the lower shell, m 2 and m 3 represent the masses of the soft-packed battery cells inside the shell. m 4 is the mass of the upper shell. The main parameter matrices in the model include the mass matrix M , the damping matrix C , the stiffness matrix K , displacement vector X and the external force vector F . According to literatures [11,26], when the SOC changes, the stiffness and volume of the internal cells of the battery will change due to the extraction and insertion of lithium ions in the positive and negative electrodes. When the SOC rises, lithium ions are detached from the positive electrode and embedded into the porous structure of the negative electrode, resulting in slight expansion of the structure of the graphite negative electrode. And compared with the negative electrode, shrinkage of volume of lithium iron phosphate positive electrode is smaller. The total volume of the battery increases. The internal stress and stiffness of the battery cell increase. When SOC decreases, the condition is opposite. Therefore, in the process of charge and discharge, the matrices C and K in the mechanical model of plastic shell battery will change due to the changes of stiffness and volume of internal cells. Therefore, SOC is related to C and K . According to the established mechanical model of the battery, the functional relationship between the excitation signal F and the displacement X of the model can be deduced. Then, the kinematic equation of the mechanical vibration characteristic model can be obtained:
(1)
{
M
X ¨
t
+ C
X ˙

+ K X

(2)

m 1

are the initial values of displacement and velocity of the mass blocks, respectively. According to Eq. (1), we try to obtain the displacement of the battery upper surface under the influence of the excitation signal F to obtain the system transfer function of the battery vibration characteristics. Based on the theory of linear differential equations, the solution type of Eq. (1) is as follow:
(3)
X = φ
e
λ t
where λ is the eigenvalue, and φ is the complex amplitude of each mass block of the system. By substituting Eq. (3) into Eq. (1), the solution of displacement X is transformed into a quadratic eigenvalue problem:
(4)
λ 2
M + λ C + K
φ = 0

The necessary and sufficient condition for Eq. (4) to obtain a nonzero solution is as follow:
(5)

M + λ C + K
= 0

Eq. (5) is a 2 ​N power algebraic equation about λ. Therefore, there are 2 ​N eigenvalues λ
r (r ​= ​1, 2, …, 2 ​N). Here, N is the degrees of freedom of the system, and for the model established in this paper, N ​= ​4. For the underdamped system with attenuated vibration, the solutions of the system are complex numbers. Since Eq. (5) is an algebraic equation with real coefficients, its complex eigenvalues must appear in conjugate pairs. Therefore, Eq. (5) has N pairs of complex conjugate eigenvalues. The r-th pair of complex conjugate eigenvalues is as follow:
(6)

Since matrix M is positive definite, matrix A should be full rank. According to Eq. (10), a

Then, the free vibration of the model can be obtained by substituting Eq. (14) into Eq. (12):
(15)

By substituting Eq. (7) and Eq. (9) into Eq. (15), the free vibration of the model under physical coordinates can be obtained:
(16)

To obtain the transfer function H(ω) of the model, it is necessary to solve the impulse response h(t) of the model. Considering that the model is initially stationary, m 1 is influenced by the unit impulse at t ​= ​0. Hence, when t ​> ​0, the initial condition of the model is as follow:
(17)

(19)

Then, by Fourier transformation of Eq. (19), the model transfer function can be obtained as follows:
(20)

j ω −
λ r

After the system transfer function is established, the mass matrix M of the model and the transfer function of the experimental object can be determined through experiments. Then, the stiffness matrix K and damping matrix C of the model are identified by the least square method with MATLAB.

It should be noted that during the experiment, LDV can obtain the vibration velocity of the upper surface of the battery, not the displacement. Therefore, it is necessary to convert Eq. (20) to achieve the matching between the model input and output and the experimental data. There is an integral relationship on the time scale between the displacement and the velocity. Therefore, the system function Eq. (20) can be transformed into Eq. (21):
(21)
H '
ω
= j ω
∑
r = 1
8
φ r
φ r T
a r
j ω −
λ r

The battery vibration test platform is shown in Fig. 3 . The signal generator can generate a sweep signal. The signal is input to the vibrator through the power amplifier to produce mechanical vibration. The vibration of the vibrator is obtained through an acceleration sensor glued at the bottom of the vibrator. The signal is collected by a high-speed data collector. Then, the data is recorded on the computer. The vibration of the upper surface of the battery is obtained by the laser Doppler vibrometer. The converted electrical signal is collected by the high-speed data collector and transmitted to the computer. In addition, a battery test system is used to charge or discharge the battery. The experimental data are directly transmitted to the computer.

The tested object is a plastic-cased LiFePO₄ battery produced by CALB. The type is LFP41//115/244-CA60F. The nominal voltage is 3.2 ​V. The nominal capacity is 60 Ah, and the nominal energy is 192 ​Wh. The total weight of the plastic-cased lithium-ion battery is 2,000 g, and the mass of the battery shell is approximately 336 ​g.

To measure the vibration characteristics of the battery under different SOCs, a small current rate battery charge and discharge experiment is set up to change the internal state of the battery. The current rate of battery charging is 0.05 C. The charging mode is CCCV. The discharge mode is CC. Before the formal experiment, the battery was charged and discharged more than 3 times to ensure its stability. During the experiment, the sweep vibration test is started every 10 ​min. The sweep frequency range is 100 ​Hz–1,200 ​Hz. The acceleration sensor and LDV collect the vibration data of the lower and upper surfaces of the battery. Fig. 4 shows the characteristic curves of vibration in different SOCs. Fig. 4 (a) is the curve of vibration during charging and Fig. 4 (b) is curve of vibration during discharging. According to Fig. 4, with the increase of SOC, the value of the peak of vibration characteristic curve decreases, while its frequency increases.

In this paper, the vibration excitation u (m/s2) applied to the battery is measured by the acceleration sensor, and the vibration velocity response w (m/s) of the battery is measured by the laser Doppler vibrometer. To obtain the mechanical vibration transfer function of the battery, it is necessary to transform the measured data in the frequency domain. The transformation method is shown in Eq. (22).
(22)
{
U = f f t
( u ×
m 0
)
W = f f t
( w )
where m
0 is the mass of the moving coil column of the vibrator, and the transfer function of battery vibration can be obtained as
H ′
( ω )
=
W U
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the vibration characteristics with different SOCs
ce:section.ce:para: With the experimental platform, the battery vibration test experiment is carried out. The mechanical vibration curves of the battery under different SOCs during charge and discharge can be measured. Then, the obtained battery vibration characteristic curve is fitted through the battery mechanical vibration model constructed in Section 2. In the process, the least squares method is used to achieve the matching between experimental data and model system function. The battery model developed in this paper is a low-order mechanical model, which only considers several parts that have a great influence on mechanical vibration. In order to maximize the validity of the model, the least square fitting method is mainly used for the three peaks of the vibration characteristic curve. According to the mechanical theory, the peaks of the vibration characteristic curve can better reflect the stiffness and damping characteristics of the battery. Therefore, the distortion of model reduction can be reduced. The damping and stiffness parameters in the model can be calculated. The fitting results of the battery vibration characteristic response curve under different SOCs during charging process are shown in Fig. 5 . The fitting results of the battery vibration characteristic response curve under different SOCs during discharging are shown in Fig. 6 . The mechanical vibration model parameters of the battery with different SOCs are shown in Table 1 .

Fig. 5 shows the comparison between the battery mechanical vibration model fitting curves and the measured battery vibration characteristic curves when SOCs ​= ​0%, 20%, 40%, 60%, 80% and 100% during battery charging. It can be seen that the overall trend of the fitting curve of the battery vibration model is the same as that of the measurement curve. At the same time, the model fairly fits the three characteristic peaks of the vibration response characteristic curve. The established battery vibration model can fairly fit the changes in battery mechanical vibration characteristics during battery charging. Through comparison, it can also be seen that the amplitude of the peak value changes under different SOCs. If SOC ​= ​0%, the amplitude of the first characteristic peak is 2.75 ​m/s/N. When SOC ​= ​100%, the amplitude of the first characteristic peak is 2.48 ​m/s/N. At the same time, the positions of the peaks also shift.

Fig. 6 shows the comparison between the battery vibration model fitting curves and the measured battery vibration response characteristic curves when SOCs ​= ​100%, 80%, 60%, 40%, 20% and 0% during battery discharge. It can be seen that during the discharging process, the fitting curve of the battery vibration model has the same overall trend as the measurement curve and can fairly fit the three peaks of the vibration characteristic curve. The change in peak amplitude shows the same trend as that in Fig. 5.

In conclusion, the battery mechanical vibration model established in this paper can fairly simulate the changes in mechanical vibration characteristics of the battery in the process of charge and discharge. Table 2 shows the relative root mean square errors (RMSEs) of the model to the vibration characteristics of the battery system. It can be seen that the mean relative errors of the model with charging and discharging data are 10.16% and 10.17%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The relationship between battery mechanical parameters and SOC
ce:section.ce:para: The previous section presents the results of fitting the characteristics of the battery by the mechanical vibration model under different SOCs. Furthermore, the change in the mechanical characteristic parameters of the battery under different SOCs can be obtained. Fig. 7 is the variation of damping parameters of battery mechanical vibration model with battery SOC. It can be seen that the damping parameters in the vibration model change regularly with the change in battery SOC. Among them, c 1, c 3 and c 5 have a certain negative correlation with the SOC of the battery, and c 2 and c 4 have a certain positive correlation with the SOC of the battery.

Fig. 8 shows the variation of the stiffness parameters in the vibration model with battery SOC. It can be seen that with the change of battery SOC during charging and discharging, there is a certain correlation between the stiffness element of the vibration model and battery SOC. Among them, k 1 and k 2 have a certain negative correlation with the SOC of the battery, and k 3, k 4 and k 5 have a certain positive correlation with the SOC of the battery.

In addition to the certain correlation between the battery vibration model parameters and the battery SOC, the change in SOC in the process of charging and discharging will cause a change in the internal structure of the battery and further cause a change in the amplitude and position of the peak value of the battery vibration characteristic curve. Similar to the parameters of the battery vibration model, the peak center position of the battery vibration response characteristic curve also has a certain correlation with the battery SOC. To reduce the error caused by noise in the measurement, the centroid of the peak value of the characteristic curve is used to represent the position of the peak value. The relationship between the peak value and battery SOC is further compared and analyzed. Fig. 9 shows the curves of the peak centroids of the battery with the SOCs of the battery. There is a positive correlation between the centroid changes of the three peaks of the battery vibration response characteristic curve and the SOC of the battery.

To further evaluate the correlation between the battery mechanical model parameters, vibration peak position parameters and battery SOC, the Spearman index is used to calculate the correlation between the parameters and battery SOC. The Spearman calculation formula is shown in Eq. (23):
(23)
ρ =
∑ i
(
x i
−
x ¯
)
(
y i
−
y ¯
)
∑ i
(
x i
−
x ¯
)
2
∑ i
(
y i
−
y ¯
)
2

The correlation between the parameters and SOC can be obtained from Eq. (23), and the calculation results are shown in Table 3 . Table 3 shows that the damping parameters in the battery mechanical vibration model have the lowest correlation with battery SOC, and the absolute value of the correlation is between 0.13 and 0.40. In addition, in the process of SOC varying, the upper and lower surfaces are subjected to the opposite force of the internal components, resulting in a negative correlation between c 1 and c 5, which is −0.60. Compared with damping parameters, the correlation between stiffness parameters and SOC is higher, among which k 4 has the highest correlation, which is 0.78, and the correlation coefficient between k 5 and SOC is 0.68. The centroid of the battery vibration response characteristic curve has a high correlation with the battery SOC. The correlation coefficient of the first peak centroid is 0.90, the correlation coefficient of the second peak centroid is 0.71, and the correlation coefficient of the third peak centroid is 0.82. Based on the calculation result, battery mechanical vibration model can be used to indicate the state changes inside the plastic-cased battery.

Considering the application of battery vibration model in this paper, there are two main application scenarios, including the state estimation of batteries on the vehicle and the performance evaluation of retired batteries. For the former, as the excitation frequency and the vibration frequency transmitted by the vehicle to the battery are not in the same frequency range [27], the external force caused by driving has little effect on the measurement of vibration characteristics. The vibration excitation can be applied by a piezoelectric transducer attached to the surface of battery. For the latter, the vibration model and the state characterization method can be used to solve the problem of rapid performance evaluation in the process of secondary utilization of the retired batteries.

In this paper, research on the vibration characteristic modeling and the state characterization of a plastic-cased lithium-ion battery is carried out. With the analysis of the physical structure of the plastic-cased battery, the battery is modeled with the first principle of the mechanical modeling method. According to the relative relationship of each model component, a four-degree-freedom mechanical vibration model of the battery is obtained. The transfer function of the battery mechanical vibration model is deduced. In order to verify the model, the battery vibration experimental system is built. The vibration response data of the battery during charging and discharging are measured by a laser Doppler vibrometer. The frequency domain conversion method is used to convert the experimental data to the frequency domain amplitude of the system transfer function. By fitting the experimental data, the parameters of the battery mechanical vibration model and system frequency domain amplitude parameters are obtained. The relative RMSEs of the frequency domain amplitude is less than 11.36%. With the analysis of the changes in the battery mechanical characteristic parameters, the effectiveness of the battery mechanical vibration model in this paper is verified. The method provides a new approach for battery nondestructive state evaluation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 52177219) and the Natural Science Foundation of Guangdong Province (2021A1515010525).
